revision.comment: [[住吉神社 (安曇野市)]] へのリダイレクトを解除しました
revision.text:
'''住吉荘'''（すみよしのしょう）は、[[信濃国]][[安曇郡]]（現在の[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]）にあった[[荘園]]。荘域は[[梓川]]と[[黒沢川]]の複合扇状地の扇端に立地していた。
== 歴史 == [[平安時代|平安]]後期の[[院政]]時代に、開発領主の[[滋野氏]]流西牧氏によって[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に[[寄進地系荘園|寄進]]されて成立し、領家職は院の女御[[高階栄子]]から[[覲子内親王|宣陽門院]]に受け継がれた。[[住吉神社 (安曇野市)|住吉神社]]を荘内十八郷の総鎮守とし、荘内に[[平福寺 (安曇野市)|平福寺]]が建立された。
[[鎌倉時代]]には、『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[文治]]2年[[3月12日 (旧暦)|3月12日]]（[[1186年]][[4月3日]]）条に[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]から[[源頼朝]]に示された「関東御知行国々内乃具未済庄々注文」にも名が見える。この頃には荘内の二木郷に一日市場や七日市場、中萱郷に六日市場などの[[六斎日|六斎市]]が立つ市場町が形成された。[[建久]]2年（1191年）には[[長講堂領]]となっており、年中行事に必要な用向きを負担した。長講堂では荘園に兵役を賦課し、宿直や門番兵を務めさせ、住吉荘からは毎月3月に3名の兵を送って楊梅門の警衛に当たった。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[建武 (日本)|建武]]2年（1335年）には南朝方の荘官である滋野氏流大妻氏から没収され、北朝方の信濃守護[[小笠原貞宗]]に地頭職が安堵され、春近領とともに守護領の中心を成した。[[室町時代]]には西牧氏・大妻氏ら滋野氏族と小笠原氏は抗争を繰り返し、[[応永]]7年（[[1400年]]）の[[大塔合戦]]の一員を成し、[[小笠原長秀]]が蓄電すると、住吉荘の地頭職は幕府の御料所となったが、同25年に将軍[[足利義持]]の[[御教書]]によって[[小笠原政康]]に還付された。[[文明 (日本)|文明]]年間の領家は[[山科言国]]であり、『[[言国卿記]]』にも当荘園に関する記述が散見する。[[戦国時代]]においても京着の年貢が1000貫文を超える大規模荘園であったた。また、複数回にわたって[[諏訪大社]]下社や[[穂高神社]]の造営役を勤仕している。
== 参考文献 == *『日本歴史地名大系 20 <small>長野県の地名</small>』（平凡社、1979年） *『国史大辞典 10』（吉川弘文館、1989年） *『[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 20 <small>長野県</small>』（角川書店、1990年）
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:すみよしのしよう}} [[Category:信濃国の荘園]] [[Category:安曇野市の歴史]] [[Category:小笠原氏]]

== 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系 20 <small>長野県の地名</small>』（平凡社、1979年） * 『国史大辞典 8』（吉川弘文館、1989年） * 『[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 20 <small>長野県</small>』（角川書店、1990年） * 『[[長野県史]] 通史編 第2巻 中世1』
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:すみよしのしよう}} [[Category:信濃国の荘園]] [[Category:安曇野市の歴史]] [[Category:小笠原氏]]

'''住吉荘'''（すみよしのしょう）は、[[信濃国]][[安曇郡]]（現在の[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]）にあった[[荘園]]。荘域は[[梓川]]と[[黒沢川]]の複合扇状地の扇端に立地していた。
== 歴史 == [[平安時代|平安]]後期の[[院政]]時代に、開発領主の[[滋野氏]]流西牧氏によって[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に[[寄進地系荘園|寄進]]されて成立し、領家職は院の女御[[高階栄子]]から[[覲子内親王|宣陽門院]]に受け継がれた後、[[山科教成]]に安堵された<ref>「講座日本荘園史 第5巻」（1989年、[[吉川弘文館]]）p.283</ref>。[[住吉神社 (安曇野市)|住吉神社]]を荘内十八郷の総鎮守とし、荘内に[[平福寺 (安曇野市)|平福寺]]が建立された。
[[鎌倉時代]]には、『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[文治]]2年[[3月12日 (旧暦)|3月12日]]（[[1186年]][[4月3日]]）条に[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]から[[源頼朝]]に示された「関東御知行国々内乃具未済庄々注文」にも名が見える。この頃には荘内の二木郷に一日市場や七日市場、中萱郷に六日市場などの[[六斎日|六斎市]]が立つ市場町が形成された。[[建久]]2年（1191年）には[[長講堂領]]となっており、年中行事に必要な用向きを負担した。長講堂では荘園に兵役を賦課し、宿直や門番兵を務めさせ、住吉荘からは毎月3月に3名の兵を送って楊梅門の警衛に当たった<ref>「長野県史 通史編 第2巻」p.400</ref>。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[建武 (日本)|建武]]2年（1335年）には南朝方の荘官である大妻氏から没収され、北朝方の信濃守護[[小笠原貞宗]]に地頭職が安堵され、春近領とともに守護領の中心を成した。[[室町時代]]には西牧氏・大妻氏ら滋野氏族と小笠原氏は抗争を繰り返し、[[応永]]7年（[[1400年]]）の[[大塔合戦]]の一員を成し、[[小笠原長秀]]が蓄電すると、住吉荘の地頭職は幕府の御料所となったが、同25年に将軍[[足利義持]]の[[御教書]]によって[[小笠原政康]]に還付された。一時期、[[伏見宮栄仁親王|栄仁親王]]・[[伏見宮貞成親王|貞成親王]]が領家となり、山科家と対立した（『[[看聞日記]]』）。[[文明 (日本)|文明]]年間の領家は[[山科言国]]であり、奉行職に源清幸を任命、年貢は為替によって代銭納されたことなど、『[[言国卿記]]』には当荘園に関する記述が散見する。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]においても京着の年貢が1000貫文を超える大規模荘園であった。また、複数回にわたって[[諏訪大社]]下社や[[穂高神社]]の造営役を勤仕している。
== 脚注 == <references/>
== 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系 20 <small>長野県の地名</small>』（平凡社、1979年） * 『国史大辞典 8』（吉川弘文館、1989年） * 『[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 20 <small>長野県</small>』（角川書店、1990年） * 『[[長野県史]] 通史編 第2巻 中世1』
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:すみよしのしよう}} [[Category:信濃国の荘園]] [[Category:安曇野市の歴史]] [[Category:小笠原氏]]

The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River (Nagano)|ja|梓川}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}. == History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|in..."
revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River (Nagano)|ja|梓川}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by {{ill|Prince Eihito of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮栄仁親王}} and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Shōen in Shinano Province]] [[Category:Shōen in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Azumino, Nagano]] [[Category:Ogasawara clan]]

The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by {{ill|Prince Eihito of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮栄仁親王}} and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River (Nagano)|ja|梓川}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by {{ill|Prince Eihito of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮栄仁親王}} and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by {{ill|Prince Eihito of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮栄仁親王}} and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by {{ill|Prince Eihito of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮栄仁親王}} and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Shōen in Shinano Province]] [[Category:Shōen in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Azumino, Nagano]] [[Category:Ogasawara clan]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi-shō]][[Category:Sumiyoshi-shō]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]]

'''住吉荘'''（すみよしのしょう）は、[[信濃国]][[安曇郡]]（現在の[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]）にあった[[荘園]]。荘域は[[梓川]]と[[黒沢川]]の複合扇状地の扇端に立地していた。
== 歴史 == [[平安時代|平安]]後期の[[院政]]時代に、開発領主の[[滋野氏]]流西牧氏によって[[後白河天皇|後白河院]]に[[寄進地系荘園|寄進]]されて成立し、領家職は院の女御[[高階栄子]]から[[覲子内親王|宣陽門院]]に受け継がれた後、[[山科教成]]に安堵された<ref>「講座日本荘園史 第5巻」（1989年、[[吉川弘文館]]）p.283</ref>。[[住吉神社 (安曇野市)|住吉神社]]を荘内十八郷の総鎮守とし、荘内に[[平福寺 (安曇野市)|平福寺]]が建立された。
[[鎌倉時代]]には、『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[文治]]2年[[3月12日 (旧暦)|3月12日]]（[[1186年]][[4月3日]]）条に[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]から[[源頼朝]]に示された「関東御知行国々内乃具未済庄々注文」にも名が見える。この頃には荘内の二木郷に一日市場や七日市場、中萱郷に六日市場などの[[六斎日|六斎市]]が立つ市場町が形成された。[[建久]]2年（1191年）には[[長講堂領]]となっており、年中行事に必要な用向きを負担した。長講堂では荘園に兵役を賦課し、宿直や門番兵を務めさせ、住吉荘からは毎月3月に3名の兵を送って楊梅門の警衛に当たった<ref>「長野県史 通史編 第2巻」p.400</ref>。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[建武 (日本)|建武]]2年（1335年）には南朝方の荘官である大妻氏から没収され、北朝方の信濃守護[[小笠原貞宗]]に地頭職が安堵され、[[春近領]]とともに守護領の中心を成した。[[室町時代]]には西牧氏・大妻氏ら滋野氏族と小笠原氏は抗争を繰り返し、[[応永]]7年（[[1400年]]）の[[大塔合戦]]の一員を成し、[[小笠原長秀]]が逐電すると、住吉荘の地頭職は幕府の御料所となったが、同25年に将軍[[足利義持]]の[[御教書]]によって[[小笠原政康]]に還付された。一時期、[[伏見宮栄仁親王|栄仁親王]]・[[伏見宮貞成親王|貞成親王]]が領家となり、山科家と対立した（『[[看聞日記]]』）。[[文明 (日本)|文明]]年間の領家は[[山科言国]]であり、奉行職に源清幸を任命、年貢は為替によって代銭納されたことなど、『[[言国卿記]]』には当荘園に関する記述が散見する。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]においても京着の年貢が1000貫文を超える大規模荘園であった。また、複数回にわたって[[諏訪大社]]下社や[[穂高神社]]の造営役を勤仕している。
== 脚注 == <references/>
== 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系 20 <small>長野県の地名</small>』（平凡社、1979年） * 『国史大辞典 8』（吉川弘文館、1989年） * 『[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 20 <small>長野県</small>』（角川書店、1990年） * 『[[長野県史]] 通史編 第2巻 中世1』
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:すみよしのしよう}} [[Category:信濃国の荘園]] [[Category:長野県域の荘園]] [[Category:安曇野市の歴史]] [[Category:小笠原氏]]

Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.

'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] to [[Category:qqqq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. '''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.

revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2年3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=draft}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗|12=draft}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=draft}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀|12=draft}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=draft}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持|12=draft}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=draft}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明|12=draft}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=draft}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=draft}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社|12=draft}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園|12=draft}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市|12=draft}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=draft}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政|12=draft}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|12=draft}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=draft}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=draft}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=draft}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=draft}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社|12=draft}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=draft}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=draft}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=draft}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗|12=draft}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=draft}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀|12=draft}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=draft}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持|12=draft}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=draft}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明|12=draft}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=draft}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=draft}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社|12=draft}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:11]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino, Nagano|ja|安曇野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|ja|梓川|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, under the {{ill|insei|ja|院政|qq=draft|qq=draft}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|ja|住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|qq=}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|ja|小笠原貞宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Sadamune|qq=}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|qq=}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Nagahide|qq=}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|qq=}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|ja|足利義持|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Yoshimochi|qq=}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|qq=}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei era|qq=}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|qq=}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|ja|穂高神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a [[:en:Manorialism|shōen]] (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi District, Shinano Province|qq=}} (modern-day [[:en:Azumino|Azumino, Nagano]], Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the [[:en:Azusa River|Azusa River]] and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|qq=}}.
== History == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], under the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|qq=}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|qq=}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|qq=}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|qq=}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)]] was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|qq=}} was built within its grounds.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the name of the estate appeared in the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|qq=}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōdō estates|qq=}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|qq=}}, [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]], becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|qq=}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]], the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|qq=}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimochi|Ashikaga Yoshimochi]].
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|qq=}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]], the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|qq=}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and [[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]].
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
