<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q85876391]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a [[:en:Manorialism|shōen]] (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi District, Shinano Province|1=Azumi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11450769}} (modern-day [[:en:Azumino|Azumino, Nagano]], Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the [[:en:Azusa River|Azusa River]] and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|1=Kurosawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678409}}.
== History == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], under the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|1=Shigeno clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564992}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|1=Takashina no Eishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673215}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|1=Kinshi-naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10523988}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|1=Yamashina no Rishige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711187}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)]] was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|1=Heifukuji (Azumino)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483459}} was built within its grounds.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the name of the estate appeared in the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]], becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|1=Battle of Ōtō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433918}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]], the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|1=Ogasawara Masayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10860032}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimochi|Ashikaga Yoshimochi]].
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]], the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Yamashina Norikuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]], the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Yamashina Norikuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and [[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]].
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q85876391]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a [[:en:Manorialism|shōen]] (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi District, Shinano Province|1=Azumi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11450769}} (modern-day [[:en:Azumino|Azumino, Nagano]], Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the [[:en:Azusa River|Azusa River]] and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|1=Kurosawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678409}}.
== History == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], under the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|1=Shigeno clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564992}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|1=Takashina no Eishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673215}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|1=Kinshi-naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10523988}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|1=Yamashina no Rishige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711187}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)]] was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|1=Heifukuji (Azumino)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483459}} was built within its grounds.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the name of the estate appeared in the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōdō estates|1=Chōkōdō estates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653662}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]], becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|1=Battle of Ōtō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433918}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]], the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|1=Ogasawara Masayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10860032}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimochi|Ashikaga Yoshimochi]].
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]], the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Yamashina Norikuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and [[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]].
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q85876391]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a [[:en:Manorialism|shōen]] (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi District, Shinano Province|1=Azumi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11450769}} (modern-day [[:en:Azumino|Azumino, Nagano]], Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the [[:en:Azusa River|Azusa River]] and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|1=Kurosawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678409}}.
== History == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], under the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|1=Shigeno clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564992}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|1=Takashina no Eishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673215}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|1=Kinshi-naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10523988}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|1=Yamashina no Rishige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711187}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)]] was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|1=Heifukuji (Azumino)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483459}} was built within its grounds.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the name of the estate appeared in the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōdō estates|1=Chōkōdō estates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653662}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]], becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|1=Battle of Ōtō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433918}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]], the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|1=Ogasawara Masayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10860032}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimochi|Ashikaga Yoshimochi]].
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]], the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Yamashina Norikuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and [[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]].
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q85876391]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a [[:en:Manorialism|shōen]] (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi District, Shinano Province|1=Azumi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11450769}} (modern-day [[:en:Azumino|Azumino, Nagano]], Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the [[:en:Azusa River|Azusa River]] and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|1=Kurosawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678409}}.
== History == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], under the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|1=Shigeno clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564992}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|1=Takashina no Eishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673215}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|1=Kinshi-naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10523988}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|1=Yamashina no Rishige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711187}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)]] was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|1=Heifukuji (Azumino)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483459}} was built within its grounds.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the name of the estate appeared in the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōdō estates|1=Chōkōdō estates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653662}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]], becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|1=Battle of Ōtō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433918}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]], the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|1=Ogasawara Masayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10860032}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimochi|Ashikaga Yoshimochi]].
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]], the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Yamashina Norikuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and [[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]].
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the [[:en:Azusa River|Azusa River]] and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|1=Kurosawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678409}}.
== History == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], under the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|1=Shigeno clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564992}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|1=Takashina no Eishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673215}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|1=Kinshi-naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10523988}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|1=Yamashina no Rishige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711187}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)]] was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|1=Heifukuji (Azumino)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483459}} was built within its grounds.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the name of the estate appeared in the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōdō estates|1=Chōkōdō estates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653662}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]], becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|1=Battle of Ōtō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433918}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]], the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|1=Ogasawara Masayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10860032}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimochi|Ashikaga Yoshimochi]].
At one point, the estate was administered by [[:en:Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|Prince Fushimi Yoshihito]] and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]], the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Yamashina Norikuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and [[:en:Hotaka Shrine|Hotaka Shrine]].
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q85876391]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=shōen}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi District, Shinano Province|1=Azumi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11450769}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|en|Azusa River|WD=Q11538700|lt=Azusa River}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|1=Kurosawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678409}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, under the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=insei}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|1=Shigeno clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564992}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|1=Takashina no Eishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673215}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|1=Kinshi-naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10523988}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|1=Yamashina no Rishige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711187}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|en|Sumiyoshi Shrine|WD=Q32512|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|1=Heifukuji (Azumino)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483459}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōdō estates|1=Chōkōdō estates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653662}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|en|Ogasawara Sadamune|WD=Q7079835|lt=Ogasawara Sadamune}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|1=Battle of Ōtō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433918}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|en|Ogasawara Nagahide|WD=Q7079827|lt=Ogasawara Nagahide}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|1=Ogasawara Masayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10860032}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|en|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|WD=Q466287|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimochi}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by {{ill|Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|en|Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|WD=Q10885328|lt=Prince Fushimi Yoshihito}} and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Yamashina Norikuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|en|Hotaka Shrine|WD=Q11597109|lt=Hotaka Shrine}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=shōen}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi District, Shinano Province|1=Azumi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11450769}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|en|Azusa River|WD=Q11538700|lt=Azusa River}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|1=Kurosawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678409}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, under the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=insei}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|1=Shigeno clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564992}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|1=Takashina no Eishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673215}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|1=Kinshi-naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10523988}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|1=Yamashina no Rishige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711187}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|en|Sumiyoshi Shrine|WD=Q32512|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|1=Heifukuji (Azumino)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483459}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōdō estates|1=Chōkōdō estates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653662}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|en|Ogasawara Sadamune|WD=Q7079835|lt=Ogasawara Sadamune}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|1=Battle of Ōtō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433918}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|en|Ogasawara Nagahide|WD=Q7079827|lt=Ogasawara Nagahide}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|1=Ogasawara Masayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10860032}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|en|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|WD=Q466287|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimochi}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by {{ill|Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|en|Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|WD=Q10885328|lt=Prince Fushimi Yoshihito}} and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Yamashina Norikuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|en|Hotaka Shrine|WD=Q11597109|lt=Hotaka Shrine}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉荘]]
'''Sumiyoshi-shō''' (住吉荘) was a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=shōen|1=Manorialism}} (manorial estate) located in {{ill|Azumi District, Shinano Province|ja|安曇郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi District, Shinano Province|1=Azumi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11450769}} (modern-day {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino, Nagano|1=Azumino}}, Nagano Prefecture). The estate was situated on the fan-shaped land formed by the confluence of the {{ill|Azusa River|en|Azusa River|WD=Q11538700|lt=Azusa River|1=Azusa River}} and {{ill|Kurosawa River (Nagano)|ja|黒沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosawa River (Nagano)|1=Kurosawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678409}}.
== History == During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, under the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=insei|1=Cloistered rule}} system, the estate was donated to {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the Nishimaki clan, a branch of the {{ill|Shigeno clan|ja|滋野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeno clan|1=Q11564992|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564992}}, who were local ruling developers. The estate’s administrative rights were passed from {{ill|Takasina Eiko|ja|高階栄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasina Eiko|1=Takashina no Eishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673215}}, one of the emperor’s consorts, to {{ill|Princess Kinshi|ja|覲子内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Kinshi|1=Kinshi-naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10523988}} (Sen'yōmon-in), and later entrusted to {{ill|Yamashina Tokonari|ja|山科教成|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokonari|1=Yamashina no Rishige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105711187}}.<ref>“Japanese Shōen Studies, Vol. 5” (1989, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan), p.283.</ref> The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|en|Sumiyoshi Shrine|WD=Q32512|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine (Azumino)|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}} was established as the general guardian shrine for the estate’s 18 villages, and {{ill|Heifukuji (Azumino)|ja|平福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heifukuji (Azumino)|1=Q11483459|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483459}} was built within its grounds.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the name of the estate appeared in the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} on the entry for {{ill|March 12, 1186|ja|文治2nd year 3月12日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 12, 1186|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At this time, markets were established in the estate’s villages, such as Ichinichi Market and Nanoka Market in Futagi Village and Muika Market in Nakakaya Village, forming bustling market towns. In 1191 (Kenkyū 2), the estate became part of the {{ill|Chōkōdō estates|ja|長講堂領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōdō estates|1=Q11653662|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653662}} and was responsible for supplying resources for annual rituals. The Chōkōdō imposed military duties on its estates, with guards stationed at Yōbai Gate, where Sumiyoshi-shō contributed three soldiers monthly.<ref>“Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2” (Nagano Prefecture, 1989), p.400.</ref>
In 1335 (Kenmu 2), during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, the estate was confiscated from its Southern Court administrator, the Ōtsuma clan, and granted to the Northern Court's {{ill|Shinano Governor|ja|信濃守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Governor|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|en|Ogasawara Sadamune|WD=Q7079835|lt=Ogasawara Sadamune|1=Ogasawara Sadamune}}, becoming a key part of the Northern Court's domain alongside Haruchika Territory. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, conflicts between the Shigeno clans, including the Nishimaki and Ōtsuma clans, and the Ogasawara clan continued. The estate participated in the {{ill|Ōtō Battle|ja|大塔合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtō Battle|1=Battle of Ōtō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433918}} in 1400 (Ōei 7). After the defeat of {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|en|Ogasawara Nagahide|WD=Q7079827|lt=Ogasawara Nagahide|1=Ogasawara Nagahide}}, the estate temporarily became an imperial domain but was returned to {{ill|Ogasawara Masayasu|ja|小笠原政康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Masayasu|1=Ogasawara Masayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10860032}} in 1425 (Ōei 25) by decree of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|en|Ashikaga Yoshimochi|WD=Q466287|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimochi|1=Ashikaga Yoshimochi}}.
At one point, the estate was administered by {{ill|Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|en|Prince_Fushimi_Yoshihito|WD=Q10885328|lt=Prince Fushimi Yoshihito|1=Prince Fushimi Yoshihito}} and {{ill|Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|ja|伏見宮貞成親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadafusa of Fushimi|1=Fushimi-no-miya Sadafusa-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885330}}, creating disputes with the Yamashina family. During the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era|1=Civilization}}, the estate was under the administration of {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|1=Q11470818|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470818}}, who appointed Minamoto Kiyoyuki as an overseer. Records in the {{ill|Norikuni-kyo Diary|ja|言国卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norikuni-kyo Diary|1=Tokikuni-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630975}} mention monetary payments of annual tributes through currency exchange. Even during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the estate’s annual tribute exceeded 1,000 kanmon, marking it as a significant estate. It also repeatedly provided labor for the construction of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} and {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|en|Hotaka Shrine|WD=Q11597109|lt=Hotaka Shrine|1=Hotaka Shrine}}.
== References == <references/>
== Further Reading == * “Historical Atlas of Nagano Prefecture” (Heibonsha, 1979) * “Japanese Historical Dictionary, Vol. 8” (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) * “Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary, Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture” (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * “Nagano Prefectural History, Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1”
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
